topic: How It Differs from the Globe Model
our_argument_summary: Section 1.3 describes the globe model's key features (oblate spheroid, geodynamo, Keplerian mechanics) and lists convergent evidence supporting it (GPS, Gaia, seismic tomography, etc.). The TLDR adds a sharper argumentative claim: where the dome model borrows globe data (WGS-84 gravity, JPL ephemerides) it is not competing but depending.

holes_found.description: TLDR claims the dome model borrows 'JPL ephemerides' but this claim is unsubstantiated. Searched all dome source files (monitor.py, pull_data.py, model.html, coordinates.html, home page) and our own wins.json — no evidence the dome model uses or references JPL ephemerides anywhere. The WGS-84 dependency is well-established (dome's own OPEN-001 acknowledges it), but the JPL ephemerides claim appears to be fabricated. A dome defender would correctly point out we are attributing a dependency that does not exist, undermining our credibility on the legitimate WGS-84 point.
holes_found.recommendation: Remove 'JPL ephemerides' from the TLDR. Replace with a second concrete example that IS documented — e.g., 'WGS-84 gravity, IGRF magnetic field models' (since the dome's monitor.py pulls from INTERMAGNET observatories that use IGRF baselines, and our Part 2b Section 2b.2 discusses this). Alternatively: 'WGS-84 coordinates, globe-derived latitude inputs' since the dome's Polaris-elevation-to-radius conversion literally IS the globe's latitude definition.

holes_found.description: TLDR/body disconnect — the TLDR makes the review's sharpest argumentative claim ('Where it borrows globe data... it is not competing — it is depending') but the body paragraph provides zero evidence for this dependency. The body is a straightforward description of the globe model with no mention of WGS-84, dome dependency, or any comparison to the dome model at all. A reader who expands the details section expecting elaboration on the dependency argument will find none.
holes_found.recommendation: Add 1-2 sentences to the body acknowledging the dome's data dependencies. Minimal example: 'The dome model's own coordinate system (Section 1.1) uses Polaris elevation — which equals geographic latitude in the globe model — as its primary input. Its OPEN-001 problem statement explicitly seeks dome-native coordinates to replace WGS-84 dependency. Until that is achieved, the dome's distance calculations inherit globe-model accuracy rather than demonstrating independent predictive power.' This grounds the TLDR's claim.

holes_found.description: For a section titled 'How It Differs from the Globe Model,' there is no actual comparison. The body describes only the globe side. It does not mention the dome model's alternative claims (disc-shaped earth, firmament at H(r), local sun at 5,733 km, local moon at 2,534 km, aetheric medium). Section 1.1 covers dome model description, but a comparison section should at minimum sketch both sides. A dome defender would say: 'You claim to compare the models but you only described yours.'
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief comparative paragraph or table mapping divergent structural claims: globe predicts oblate spheroid → dome predicts flat disc; globe predicts sun at 150M km → dome predicts 5,733 km; globe predicts geodynamo → dome predicts copper firmament cavity. This makes the section actually do what its title promises. Keep it brief — the details belong in later sections.

holes_found.description: The TLDR says 'Keplerian orbital mechanics' are verified by GPS. Strictly, GPS requires relativistic corrections (both special and general relativity) beyond Keplerian mechanics — ~38 microseconds/day clock drift without them. 'Keplerian' understates the theoretical framework. A nitpicky defender could say 'GPS disproves Kepler too, since Keplerian mechanics alone gets the wrong answer.'
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'Keplerian orbital mechanics' to 'orbital mechanics (Keplerian + relativistic corrections)' or simply 'orbital mechanics verified by GPS.' The relativistic correction is actually a stronger argument for us — it shows the globe framework has layers of internal consistency the dome cannot replicate.

holes_found.description: The body says 'The atmosphere transitions into a conductive ionosphere at 80–400 km altitude.' This is the correct range for the ionosphere's D/E/F layers, but it's an oversimplification that a defender could nitpick. The ionosphere isn't a sharp 'transition' — it has a complex altitude-dependent profile (D layer at 60-90 km, E at 90-150 km, F at 150-800+ km). The stated 80-400 km range actually misses the D layer starting at ~60 km. However, this is minor since the section is an overview.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional: change to '60–800 km' or '~60–400+ km' for accuracy, or simply say 'above ~60 km altitude.' Not critical for the section's purpose.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome model's Polaris elevation → radial distance conversion IS the globe model's latitude under a different name. In the globe model, Polaris elevation = observer's latitude. The dome model takes this same measured angle and feeds it through H(r)/tan(elevation) to get radius. The input is globe-derived. This is the single strongest demonstration of dependency and should be highlighted in the comparison section.
sources: raw-text/04-coordinates.txt: r(city) = solve numerically: r × tan(polaris_elevation) = H(r)
raw-text/05-model.txt: OPEN-001: Dome-native coordinates without WGS84 (IN PROGRESS)

stronger_arguments.description: The dome model's own 'Open Problems' page (OPEN-001) explicitly concedes WGS-84 dependency and lists dome-native coordinates as an unsolved problem. Citing this in Section 1.3 would make the 'depending not competing' claim self-evidently true — it's the dome's own admission.

The section could note that the globe model's predictions are independently verified across domains that share no common instrumentation: GPS (radio signals + atomic clocks), gravimetry (falling masses), seismic tomography (earthquake waves), Gaia (optical parallax), lunar laser ranging (reflected laser pulses). The convergence of independent methods is the epistemic strength the dome would need to match — not just individual data points, but cross-domain agreement.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model does genuinely attempt to describe a physically distinct cosmology — not just 'the globe is wrong' but 'here is the alternative geometry.' It has specific parameters (disc radius, firmament height function, local celestial bodies), a coordinate system, and it tests predictions against real data from INTERMAGNET, NOAA, etc. This structural completeness is unusual among alternative cosmology models and makes the comparison section warranted.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The structural completeness exposes the dependency problem more starkly than a vague alternative would. The dome model needs real-world coordinates to make predictions, and its only method of obtaining them (Polaris elevation) IS the globe model's latitude measurement. The more quantitative the dome gets, the more it reveals its parasitic relationship with globe-derived data.

deeper_analysis: Section 1.3 has a structural identity crisis. The TLDR is actually a sophisticated argumentative claim (dependency vs. competition) that belongs as the section's culminating point. The body is a purely descriptive paragraph about the globe model that reads like a textbook introduction. These serve different purposes and don't connect. The fix is straightforward: (1) keep the descriptive globe paragraph, (2) add a brief dome-model paragraph for the comparison the title promises, (3) add 1-2 sentences grounding the TLDR's dependency claim with concrete evidence (OPEN-001, Polaris-as-latitude). The JPL ephemerides reference must be removed or substantiated — it's the kind of unforced error that a dome defender would use to discredit our rigor. The WGS-84 point alone is devastating enough and is backed by the dome model's own admissions.

citations_failed: JPL ephemerides — no evidence dome model uses JPL data anywhere in source material or code
citations_unchecked: Gaia astrometry of 1.8 billion stars (correct per DR3, not checked against latest DR4)
GPS orbital mechanics at 20,000 km (correct range: actual is ~20,200 km)
lunar laser ranging (well-established, not checked for specific claims)
